meta.title: Nephrology Literature Update
meta.query: Clinical nephrology — RCTs, systematic reviews & meta-analyses (humans, English)
meta.source: Generated by Google NotebookLM, grounded in PubMed (NLM) abstracts. Each entry links to its PubMed record and DOI.
meta.note: Summaries and PICO are NotebookLM analyses of the source abstracts; criteria marked “not specified” were not stated.
meta.appraisal_source: Professor critical appraisal (clinical bottom-line, relevance, caveat).

articles.authors: Tuttle KR, Bakris GL, Baeres FMM, Bang CN, Bax WA et al.
articles.title: Kidney and Survival Benefits of Semaglutide in Diabetes With Chronic Kidney Disease: FLOW Trial Cardiovascular Subgroup Analyses
articles.summary: Semaglutide reduced the primary kidney outcome risk (eGFR decline, failure, or death) in patients with T2D and CKD across all cardiovascular risk subgroups, including those with ASCVD (HR 0.80, 95% CI 0.63-1.02) and heart failure (HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.49-0.93) . It also consistently reduced risks of all-cause death regardless of established cardiovascular disease or risk score (P for interaction > 0.60) . Numbers needed to treat to prevent one primary kidney outcome at 3 years were 22 for the ASCVD subgroup and 13 for the heart failure subgroup .

pico.population: Participants with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease stratified by cardiovascular status
pico.outcome: Primary composite kidney outcome (eGFR decline ≥50%, eGFR <15, dialysis, transplant, or death) and all-cause death

inclusion: type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease

appraisal.bottom_line: In T2D + CKD, semaglutide cut the composite kidney outcome across all CV strata — HF HR 0.67 (0.49–0.93), ASCVD HR 0.80 (0.63–1.02); 3-yr NNT 13 (HF) and 22 (ASCVD).
appraisal.relevance: Reinforces GLP-1RA as a core cardiorenal agent in diabetic CKD regardless of CV phenotype; supports use alongside SGLT2i/ARB.
appraisal.caveat: Post-hoc subgroup analysis; ASCVD CI crosses 1.0 — interaction-driven, not powered per subgroup.

articles.authors: Dos Santos Borges R, Lara Santos R, Haikal de Paula L, Borges Martins JM, Machado Gomes de Sousa PA et al.
articles.title: Efficacy, safety, and biomarker changes of B-cell activating factor and A proliferation-inducing ligand-targeted therapies in IgA nephropathy: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
articles.summary: BAFF- or APRIL-targeted therapies significantly reduced 24-hour UPCR (MD -38.94%; 95% CI -58.98 to -18.90) and significantly improved eGFR (MD 7.05 mL/min/1.73 m²; 95% CI 3.83 to 10.27) compared to placebo in patients with IgAN . Treatment also led to significant decreases in serum Gd-IgA1, IgG, IgA, and IgM, indicating reduced immune complex formation . The incidence of adverse events did not differ significantly between the study groups .

pico.outcome: Change in 24-hour UPCR, eGFR, adverse events, and serum immunoglobulin levels

inclusion: randomized controlled trials comparing BAFF- or APRIL-targeted drugs with placebo

appraisal.bottom_line: Reduced 24-h UPCR (MD −38.9%, −59.0 to −18.9) and raised eGFR (+7.05 mL/min/1.73m²), with falls in Gd-IgA1; AEs comparable to placebo.
appraisal.relevance: Adds momentum to the B-cell/plasma-cell axis (sibeprenlimab, atacicept) as disease-modifying IgAN therapy beyond supportive care.
appraisal.caveat: Few small RCTs, surrogate endpoints (proteinuria), short follow-up — no hard kidney-failure data yet.

articles.authors: Møller M, Andersen TH, Borg R, Christensen R, Bressendorff I et al.
articles.title: Complication rates of 16- and 18-gauge needles for native kidney biopsies: a systematic review and proportional meta-analysis
articles.summary: This analysis of 22,208 adult native kidney biopsies found no significant difference in major complication rates between 16G needles (1.83%, 95% CI 1.20-2.79) and 18G needles (1.29%, 95% CI 0.78-2.13) . Mean glomerular yield was similar for both sizes, at 18.8 for 16G and 17.5 for 18G needles . Meta-regression identified higher complication rates in studies with a higher prevalence of AKI, lower mean eGFR, or lower mean hemoglobin .

pico.outcome: Major complication rates, diagnostic yield (glomerular count), and study-level predictors of complications

inclusion: randomized trials and cohort studies of real-time ultrasound-guided native kidney biopsies in adults

appraisal.bottom_line: No difference in major complications (16G 1.83% vs 18G 1.29%) or glomerular yield (18.8 vs 17.5) across 22,208 biopsies.
appraisal.relevance: Reassuring — needle choice can favor operator preference; higher complications track with AKI, low eGFR, low Hb.
appraisal.caveat: Mostly single-arm cohorts → indirect study-level comparison, retrospective, heterogeneous.

articles.authors: Kelly DM, Kelleher EM, Rothwell PM
articles.title: Acute Kidney Injury and Risk of Adverse Neurocognitive Outcomes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
articles.summary: AKI was consistently associated with increased risk of stroke (adjusted HR 1.35, 95% CI 1.20-1.52), delirium (adjusted OR 1.76, 95% CI 1.42-2.17), and dementia (adjusted HR 1.64, 95% CI 1.41-1.89) . A risk gradient across AKI stages was observed for stroke, with combined stages 2 and 3 showing an HR of 1.57 (95% CI 1.35-1.81) . AKI was also linked to higher 90-day poststroke mortality (pooled HR 4.81, 95% CI 2.55-9.08) and 90-day disability .

pico.outcome: Stroke, delirium, dementia, mortality, and disability

inclusion: studies reporting quantitative estimates with measures of precision for the association between AKI and delirium, stroke, or dementia in adult populations

appraisal.bottom_line: AKI raised risk of stroke (aHR 1.35), delirium (aOR 1.76), dementia (aHR 1.64), and 90-day post-stroke mortality (HR 4.81).
appraisal.relevance: Defines an 'AKI–brain axis'; flag AKI survivors for cognitive/cerebrovascular surveillance in follow-up.
appraisal.caveat: Observational, associative only; residual confounding and reverse causality likely.

articles.authors: Abdel-Rahman SM, Mizrak B, Narin AE, Guldan M, Yilmaz ZY et al.
articles.title: The impact of pre-transplant atherosclerosis and coronary artery disease on cardiovascular and graft outcomes in kidney transplant recipients: a systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.summary: Pre-transplant coronary artery disease (CAD) in kidney transplant recipients was associated with significantly higher all-cause mortality (HR 1.68, 95% CI 1.38-2.06) and post-transplant cardiovascular events (HR 2.78, 95% CI 2.00-3.86) . Graft failure risk was also modestly but significantly increased in those with pre-existing CAD (HR 1.09, 95% CI 1.03-1.16) . These findings underscore the importance of enhanced cardiovascular risk assessment in transplant candidates .

pico.outcome: All-cause mortality, post-transplant major cardiovascular events, and graft failure

inclusion: studies reporting outcomes in kidney transplant recipients with and without pre-existing CAD

appraisal.bottom_line: Pre-existing CAD raised all-cause mortality (HR 1.68), post-KT CV events (HR 2.78), and modestly graft failure (HR 1.09).
appraisal.relevance: Supports rigorous pre-transplant cardiac risk stratification in candidates.
appraisal.caveat: Observational pooling; CAD definitions / revascularization status heterogeneous.

articles.authors: Tear AA, Fauziah N, Riswari SF, Purba AR, Akhlaq MM et al.
articles.title: Prognostic factors in malaria patients with acute kidney injury: a systematic review and meta analysis
articles.summary: Key predictors of mortality in malaria patients with AKI included ventilator requirement (OR 30.35), respiratory distress syndrome (OR 18.50), and hypotension (OR 18.23) . Significant biochemical predictors of death included acidosis (OR 4.88), hyperkalemia (OR 3.99), and hyponatremia (OR 3.67) . Other factors like neurological involvement, DIC, and jaundice were also strongly associated with increased mortality .

pico.population: Malaria patients with acute kidney injury

inclusion: observational studies that showed the association between clinical or laboratory abnormalities and mortality

appraisal.bottom_line: Mortality predictors: ventilator need (OR 30.4), ARDS (18.5), hypotension (18.2), acidosis (4.9), hyperkalemia (4.0).
appraisal.relevance: Niche but useful triage in endemic settings — markers of severe/multiorgan disease drive death.
appraisal.caveat: 16 observational studies, n=1,104; predictors mostly reflect overall severity, not AKI-specific.

articles.authors: Fan HH, Yuan H, Liu B, Liu HY, Chen SQ et al.
articles.title: Development and evaluation of a home-based exercise intervention for frail kidney transplant recipients using the ADOPT model
articles.summary: A structured home-based exercise program (ADOPT model) significantly improved grip strength, gait speed, physical performance scores, and quality of life in frail kidney transplant recipients (P < .05) . Significant reductions in anxiety and depression were also observed in the intervention group (P < .01) . No significant changes in serum creatinine were found in either group, suggesting safety for renal function .

pico.intervention: Home-based exercise program based on the ADOPT model
pico.comparison: Standard postoperative care with self-directed home exercise
pico.outcome: Fried Frailty Phenotype, grip strength, gait speed, quality of life, anxiety/depression, and serum creatinine

inclusion: meeting diagnostic criteria for frailty (Fried phenotype)
undergoing kidney transplantation for the first time
normal listening, speaking, reading, and writing abilities

appraisal.bottom_line: Improved grip strength, gait speed, QoL, and anxiety/depression (P<.05); no change in creatinine.
appraisal.relevance: Low-cost prehab/rehab adjunct for frail transplant recipients.
appraisal.caveat: Single-center, small, unblinded, soft/functional endpoints; age capped ≤65.

articles.authors: Costa TA, de Sousa LMT, Nogueira A, Chagas GCL, Abreu M et al.
articles.title: Association between SGLT2 inhibitors and outcomes after heart transplant in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.summary: SGLT2i use in heart transplant recipients with T2D was associated with a significantly lower risk of all-cause mortality (adjusted HR 0.69, 95% CI 0.51-0.93) . There was no significant increase in the risk of urinary tract infections (OR 0.77, 95% CI 0.14-4.05) . Most included studies also reported stable or increased eGFR from baseline in patients receiving SGLT2i .

pico.population: Heart transplant (HTX) recipients with type 2 diabetes
pico.outcome: All-cause mortality, urinary tract infection (UTI) incidence, and eGFR change from baseline

inclusion: studies of HTX recipients with T2D comparing outcomes in SGLT2i users vs. non-users

appraisal.bottom_line: SGLT2i linked to lower all-cause mortality (aHR 0.69, 0.51–0.93), no excess UTI, stable/improved eGFR.
appraisal.relevance: Extends SGLT2i cardiorenal benefit to the immunosuppressed heart-transplant population.
appraisal.caveat: Observational, small pooled n; infection / euglycemic-DKA safety underpowered (UTI CI 0.14–4.05).
